original_abstract: Skeletal editing has emerged as a transformative frontier in synthetic organic chemistry, enabling the precise, atom-level modification of molecular cores. By facilitating the insertion, deletion, or exchange of individual atoms within a pre-existing scaffold, this "atom-level surgery" offers a streamlined alternative to resource-intensive de novo synthesis and provides a powerful tool for late-stage functionalization. This review highlights recent breakthroughs in the skeletal editing of carbo- and heterocyclic systems, with a particular emphasis on single-atom manipulations. We categorize the field into three primary strategic pillars: ring expansion via atom insertion, ring contraction through atom deletion, and transmutation via atom exchange. By integrating historical foundations with cutting-edge developments from the last five years—including carbene-mediated carbon insertions, nitrene-driven nitrogen scanning, and photochemical rearrangements—this evaluation explores the diverse mechanisms and insertive agents currently driving the field. Furthermore, we discuss the burgeoning area of stereochemical editing and isotopic exchange. By consolidating these advances, this review provides a holistic perspective on the current capabilities and future challenges of skeletal editing in the pursuit of molecular diversity for medicinal chemistry and materials science.
humanized_abstract: Although diverse strategies to obtain skeletal diversity at the level of the molecular core (i.e., the framework that connects all other features in a molecule) have been known for many years, skeletal editing, the technology through which individual features such as single atoms are introduced, removed, or substituted within the molecular framework in an efficient fashion and that has come to be known as atom-level surgery, has recently become a main topic in synthetic organic chemistry. Skeletal editing has proven to be extremely efficient for preparing complex molecules, facilitating late stage functionalization, while significantly reducing the amount of material necessary for synthesis (i.e., far less than is needed for the construction of the same molecule from first principles). In this review, based on more recent research on skeletal editing of both carbocyclics and heterocyclics at an atom editing level (i.e., where individual atoms are introduced/removed/replaced in a precursor architecture), we start with a classification of skeletal editing into three main pillars of ring expansion via atom insertion, ring contraction through atom removal, and ring transmutation through atom exchange. We then focus on an exhaustive survey, essentially exclusively based on findings in situ single-track damage analytical data from the last five years to the best of our knowledge, that synthesizes the historical basis provided by recent achievements, using as an example several hundreds of examples of carbene-based atom insertions that have been documented during the last decades and the increasing amount of more recent nitrene-based nitrogen-scanning data, while adding an ever-increasing body of photochemical rearrangements and other insertive-mode reactions. We finally touch upon other emerging examples within skeletal editing, including that of stereochemical editing (i.e., of chirality-bearing atoms and functional groups) and isotopic editing.
